task:
You are taking over Nimbus “Minimal Report UI V1” implementation.

Context:
- Product direction: keep GitHub/CI-native workflow, but add a lightweight visual report viewer.
- We do NOT want a full dashboard yet.
- We want an easy shareable/readable report page with strong copy-to-agent UX.
- Spec reference: llm-docs/minimal-report-ui-v1.md


Your mission:
Build a minimal report viewer experience end-to-end on a throwaway branch with sensible defaults.


Non-negotiable goals:
1) Render a review by reviewId from existing worker API.
2) Make it easy to copy report content into coding agents.
3) Keep setup simple and avoid overbuilding.


Implementation defaults (already decided):
- URL route: /reports/:reviewId
- Data source: existing API GET /api/reviews/:id
- V1 has no auth/SSO complexity (public-by-reviewId is acceptable for now)
- Show:
  - summary header (recommendation, risk, findings count, status, timestamps)
  - findings cards
  - evidence list
  - rendered markdown summary
  - raw JSON (collapsible)
- Copy actions:
  - copy full markdown
  - copy full JSON
  - copy per-finding text
  - copy per-finding “fix prompt”
- Use this fix prompt template per finding:


  You are helping fix a Nimbus code review finding.


  Finding title: <title>
  Severity: <severity>
  Confidence: <confidence>
  Description:
  <description>


  Conditions:
  <conditions or "none provided”>


  Locations:
  <path:line list>


  Suggested fix:
  <suggestedFix or "not provided”>


  Evidence refs:
  <evidence refs list>


  Please:
  1) Propose a minimal safe code change.
  2) Explain why it resolves the issue.
  3) List any tests to run.
  4) Return a patch-style diff when possible.


- UX defaults:
  - neutral clean style, readability-first
  - toast feedback on copy (“Copied”)
  - robust loading/empty/error states
- Technical defaults:
  - use existing web stack if present in repo; if absent, choose lightweight React+Vite
  - sanitize markdown rendering
  - static-host-friendly arch
Can you give me a report id real quick I can test with?
Go through the entire process to make me a workspace with a deployment id and create a review and give me the review id
user_feedback_text: Go through the entire process to make me a workspace with a deployment id and create a review and give me the review id

task:
okay I want us to really think about our review approach. There are 4 really important things to consider:

1. Context retrievial.
Right now we should be able to review code based on diffs right? That’s about it. We should be able to see/review the full file, not just the changes themselves. We should also be examining related files. Tracking down things the changed code calls or is called by. Then there’s repo-level conventions, we might want to take into consideration agent.md files or something. Maybe we look for a specific file for us? code-review.md? Linkting configs, is another one, then also past PR concerns (maybe circle back to this one later).

Are we able to make some sort of graph of the repo (based on Entire Checkpoints) for better retrieval?

2. The review prompt and output structure
 We probably want to do separate passes and focus on really specific things related to the code changes. Stuff like securtity, logic correctness, style/conventions, breaking change detection. Perhaps we can deploy sub agents to do these reviews and have them give a final output in the end?

3. We don’t have a feedback learning loop right now, however, while I think this is really important, this feels like a pretty big feature. We should keep this in mind but circle back to it later.

4. The integration surface
Right now most review bots are showing up as comments in PRs, IDE plugins or from CLIs. As of right now we don’t have any of these integrations. If I wanted a user to test this out right now they’d go through hell setting up all the Cloudflare infra on their own accounts. I do like how we have an interface where we can just provide a URL to show the review results that make it easy to copy/paste into local LLms.

I think we should work on 1,2, and 4. I’d feel way more confident sharing when these are in a good place. Is there anything you think I’m missing from my thoughts regarding quality? Would all of that help you put together a decent plan for what we do as far
user_feedback_text:

1. Context retrievial.
Right now we should be able to review code based on diffs right? That’s about it. We should be able to see/review the full file, not just the changes themselves. We should also be examining related files. Tracking down things the changed code calls or is called by. Then there’s repo-level conventions, we might want to take into consideration agent.md files or something. Maybe we look for a specific file for us? code-review.md? Linkting configs, is another one, then also past PR concerns (maybe circle back to this one later).

Are we able to make some sort of graph of the repo (based on Entire Checkpoints) for better retrieval?

2. The review prompt and output structure
 We probably want to do separate passes and focus on really specific things related to the code changes. Stuff like securtity, logic correctness, style/conventions, breaking change detection.

Also I like how when I run the review tool with you, you update me on if further reviews might be overdoing it due to edge cases and what not, we’re hitting a point of “dimishing returns”, we should keep that in frame as well.
- Why would high-sev findings fail CI? It should just be showing that it’s a high sev issue.

Don’tmake the execution plan yet, consider my responses and ask more questions if needed.

task:
Let’s go ahead start specing out how we want to build out the context retrieval part of @llm-docs/review-quality-phases-context-prompt-integration.md .

We're specing out Phase 1 of Nimbus review quality improvements: Context Retrieval.

The goal is that review quality should not depend on diff hunks alone. We're introducing a `ReviewContext` type that gets assembled at `review create` time and passed to the review agent.


**Hard constraint**: Nimbus only supports Entire checkpoints. There is no fallback to static analysis or generic repo scanning. The co-change signal from Entire session history is the core retrieval mechanism, not an enhancement.


**What ReviewContext should contain:**


- Diff hunks (baseline, always present)
- Full text of changed files
- Related files derived from co-change frequency in prior Entire checkpoint sessions — given the set of changed files in this review, scan recent checkpoint sessions on the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch, find sessions that touched any of those files, collect all other files touched in those sessions, rank by co-change frequency, include top N
- Convention/guidance files discovered by walking from each changed file's directory up to repo root, matching a known pattern list (AGENTS.md, CODE_REVIEW.md, .eslintrc*, biome.json, pyproject.toml, etc.)
- Checkpoint context from the Entire session linked to this commit: sessionIntent (from prompt.txt or context.md), agentType, and attribution trailer


**Token budget**: No budget by default. Include everything and let the model's context window be the natural hard ceiling. If a user optionally sets a budget via config and the assembled ReviewContext exceeds it, hard fail with a clear message telling them to increase the budget. Do not silently degrade or drop context. Token usage is tracked in the provenance manifest as a feedback signal — large token usage indicates a large commit, not a tool configuration problem.


**Cloudflare platform specifics:**


- **ReviewCont
user_feedback_text:

task:
Update the Phase 1 spec with these three changes:

1. **D1 cache composite key** — change `review_cochange_cache` to use a composite primary key of `(file_path, repo)` instead of `file_path` alone. Add `repo TEXT NOT NULL` column (value is the owner/name slug, e.g. "dayhaysoos/nimbus"). Update the CREATE TABLE statement and any references to cache lookup/write logic accordingly.


2. **Session eligibility definition** — in Section 3, clarify that "eligible checkpoint sessions" means the N most recent sessions ordered by `assembledAt` timestamp descending. No commit ancestry traversal in Phase 1.


3. **Convention file cap** — change `conventionFileMaxCount` default from 50 to 10 in Section 8 and anywhere else it's referenced.


No other changes.
user_feedback_text:
Update the Phase 1 spec with these three changes:

1. **D1 cache composite key** — change `review_cochange_cache` to use a composite primary key of `(file_path, repo)` instead of `file_path` alone. Add `repo TEXT NOT NULL` column (value is the owner/name slug, e.g. "dayhaysoos/nimbus"). Update the CREATE TABLE statement and any references to cache lookup/write logic accordingly.


2. **Session eligibility definition** — in Section 3, clarify that "eligible checkpoint sessions" means the N most recent sessions ordered by `assembledAt` timestamp descending. No commit ancestry traversal in Phase 1.


3. **Convention file cap** — change `conventionFileMaxCount` default from 50 to 10 in Section 8 and anywhere else it's referenced.


No other changes.

task: You can start building now. Make sure you build in a sensible, feature based way. I like to think in terms of vertical slices. When you’re done with a vertical slice, review the code and run tests to make sure they pass. Fix what’s needed. Do not move on to the next slice until everything is green.

When you’re done with a vertical slice, review the code and run tests to make sure they pass. Fix what’s needed. Do not move on to the next slice until everything is green.

task:
One issue to address as a follow-up slice: the co-change soft-skip behavior (skipping co-change when repo slug is missing rather than hard-failing) is a deviation from the Phase 1 spec. The spec explicitly states that co-change from Entire checkpoint history is the primary retrieval mechanism, not optional infrastructure. Silently skipping it when repo slug is unresolvable is the same tiered degradation pattern we deliberately rejected during spec design.

Fix: make repo slug required for review context assembly. If it cannot be resolved from deployment provenance or env, hard-fail with a clear error code (consistent with the existing `unsupported_without_entire_checkpoint_context` pattern). Wire repo slug end-to-end from deploy provenance so it is always present by the time review context assembly runs.


No silent partial context. If co-change can't run, the review shouldn't proceed.
user_feedback_text:
One issue to address as a follow-up slice: the co-change soft-skip behavior (skipping co-change when repo slug is missing rather than hard-failing) is a deviation from the Phase 1 spec. The spec explicitly states that co-change from Entire checkpoint history is the primary retrieval mechanism, not optional infrastructure. Silently skipping it when repo slug is unresolvable is the same tiered degradation pattern we deliberately rejected during spec design.

Fix: make repo slug required for review context assembly. If it cannot be resolved from deployment provenance or env, hard-fail with a clear error code (consistent with the existing `unsupported_without_entire_checkpoint_context` pattern). Wire repo slug end-to-end from deploy provenance so it is always present by the time review context assembly runs.


No silent partial context. If co-change can't run, the review shouldn't proceed.
